name: Ebola Origin
description: The Ebola virus disease (EVD) has a complex history and origin, with mainstream science tracing it to zoonotic transmission from bats to humans in specific regions of Central and West Africa. However, alternative theories, often rooted in conspiracy beliefs, propose different origins. Some theories suggest that Ebola was deliberately created as a biological weapon, either by Western powers or by African governments for population control or other nefarious purposes. These theories often cite a distrust of pharmaceutical companies and Western intervention in Africa. Evidence cited includes anecdotal accounts, misinterpreted scientific data, and the perceived lack of transparency surrounding the virus's research and outbreaks. Mainstream science strongly refutes these claims, pointing to extensive phylogenetic studies demonstrating the virus's natural evolution and origin in bats. They emphasize the lack of credible evidence supporting deliberate creation, and highlight the significant efforts by international health organizations and African governments to combat Ebola outbreaks and conduct research to understand and prevent future occurrences. Critiques also focus on the perpetuation of harmful stereotypes and the undermining of public health efforts by spreading misinformation and distrust in scientific institutions. Another alternative theory suggests that Ebola is linked to ancient African curses or spiritual practices. While this is a cultural belief in some regions affected by Ebola, there is no scientific backing for this claim. Mainstream health organizations stress that relying on such beliefs hinders effective disease control and treatment efforts.
subtitle: Conspiracy and Science surrounding Ebola's Beginnings

claims.claim: Ebola was created as a biological weapon.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts, alleged leaked documents, distrust of Western powers and pharmaceutical companies.
claims.criticism: Phylogenetic studies demonstrate natural evolution from bats; lack of credible evidence for deliberate creation; perpetuates harmful stereotypes.

claims.claim: Ebola outbreaks are caused by government-sponsored population control programs.
claims.evidence: Distrust of government, claims of forced vaccinations, perceived lack of transparency.
claims.criticism: Lack of evidence; undermines public health efforts; contradicts scientific understanding of viral transmission.

claims.claim: Ebola is a result of ancient African curses or spiritual practices.
claims.evidence: Traditional beliefs in affected regions, anecdotal stories.
claims.criticism: No scientific basis; hinders effective disease control and treatment; promotes misinformation.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Leonard Horowitz
keyFigures.role: Promoter of conspiracy theories about Ebola's origin.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Peter Piot
keyFigures.role: Co-discoverer of the Ebola virus and researcher of its origins.

keyFigures.name: Dr. David Quammen
keyFigures.role: Science writer and author of 'Spillover', which investigates the zoonotic origins of Ebola.

mythologyConnections: Connections to traditional African beliefs about disease and misfortune
Belief in curses and supernatural causes of illness

timeline.event: First recognized Ebola outbreak in Yambuku, Zaire (now Democratic Republic of Congo).
Continued research into Ebola's origins and transmission; ongoing debate about alternative theories.

sources.author: Quammen, David
sources.work: Spillover: Animal Infections and the Next Human Pandemic
sources.title: Quammen, David

sources.author: Horowitz, Leonard
sources.work: Emerging Viruses: AIDS & Ebola – Nature, Accident or Intentional?
